title_seed: What I learned while deploying ML models with FastAPI and Docker
experience_points: Deployment is more than just creating an API endpoint
Input validation with Pydantic catches problems before they reach the model
Docker keeps the environment stable across machines and CI
Health check endpoints matter when running in containers
mistakes: I ignored proper error handling at first and the API crashed silently
I did not pin library versions in requirements.txt and the container broke weeks later
I forgot to add a /health endpoint and had no way to check if the container was alive
lessons: Production readiness needs validation, logging, and versioning from day one
Always test your Docker image locally before pushing to any registry
Your model artifact path should come from an environment variable, not hardcoded
optional_code_snippet_idea: simple FastAPI prediction endpoint with Pydantic input model and error handling

title_seed: How MLflow changed the way I track ML experiments
experience_points: Before MLflow I was saving metrics in print statements and notebooks
MLflow autolog works for sklearn and XGBoost out of the box
The model registry lets you move models from staging to production with a single API call
Comparing runs side by side in the UI saves hours of manual spreadsheet work
mistakes: I ran 50 experiments without naming them and could not figure out which was best
I did not log the random seed and could not reproduce my best result
I logged too many metrics and the UI became cluttered
lessons: Log the essentials: params, metrics, model artifact, and dataset version
Use meaningful run names
Reproducibility requires logging everything that affects the output
optional_code_snippet_idea: mlflow.start_run() block with param logging, metric logging, and model registration

title_seed: Mistakes I made while training my first ML model
experience_points: I trained on the full dataset without a proper train/test split
I did not check for data leakage and got suspiciously high accuracy
Scaling features made a huge difference for models like SVM and KNN
I used accuracy as the only metric on an imbalanced dataset
mistakes: My test set contained rows that influenced the scaler fit
I never looked at the confusion matrix
I tuned hyperparameters on the test set instead of cross-validation
lessons: Always split data before any preprocessing step
Use multiple metrics for imbalanced problems
Cross-validation is not optional
optional_code_snippet_idea: sklearn Pipeline with StandardScaler, train_test_split, and cross_val_score

title_seed: Why model monitoring matters more than model training
experience_points: A model that performs well in testing can silently degrade in production
Data drift is the most common reason models stop working after deployment
Prometheus and Grafana give real-time visibility into model behavior
Prediction distribution monitoring caught a data pipeline bug early
mistakes: I thought deployment was the finish line with no monitoring
I only monitored infrastructure metrics, not model-specific ones
I had no alerting and found out about drift from a user complaint
lessons: Monitor input data distributions, not just model outputs
Set up alerting on day one of deployment
Treat model monitoring as a first-class engineering concern
optional_code_snippet_idea: custom Prometheus counter and histogram for prediction tracking in FastAPI

title_seed: How I set up a CI/CD pipeline for ML models using GitHub Actions
experience_points: GitHub Actions is free for public repos
I automated model validation checks before any merge to main
The pipeline runs unit tests, linting, and a smoke test on the model artifact
GitHub Secrets kept credentials out of the codebase
mistakes: I committed my .env file to the repo early on
My workflow had no caching and took 8 minutes per run
I did not test the workflow on a feature branch first
lessons: Use actions/cache for pip dependencies
Always test workflows on a branch before merging
A failing CI pipeline is better than a broken deployment
optional_code_snippet_idea: GitHub Actions workflow with pip cache, pytest run, and Docker build step

title_seed: What I learned about feature engineering that no tutorial tells you
experience_points: Good features matter more than a fancy model
Domain knowledge beats any automated feature selection algorithm
Interaction features reveal patterns tree models miss
Target encoding outperformed one-hot on high-cardinality columns
mistakes: I one-hot encoded a column with 500 unique values
I created features using the full dataset before splitting
I spent days on features when the real problem was bad data quality
lessons: Do EDA before engineering features
Simpler features that make business sense often beat complex ones
Track which features actually help using feature importance
optional_code_snippet_idea: pandas pipeline for target encoding with cross-validation to prevent leakage

title_seed: Running background tasks in Python with Celery and Redis
experience_points: Celery decouples heavy work from your web request cycle
Redis as a broker is the easiest setup
Task chaining builds pipelines where each step triggers the next
Flower gives real-time queue visibility
mistakes: I forgot to configure task serialization and got silent failures
I ran workers with default concurrency and overloaded a VM
I did not set task timeouts and a hung task blocked the queue for hours
lessons: Always set task_time_limit and soft_time_limit
Log task IDs for production tracing
Test tasks as regular Python functions first
optional_code_snippet_idea: Celery task definition with retry logic, timeout, and result backend configuration

title_seed: How I structured a real ML project from scratch
experience_points: Flat scripts fall apart when you add a second teammate
Separating data loading, feature engineering, and modeling made refactoring easy
Config files replaced hardcoded paths
A proper .gitignore saved me from committing 2 GB of data files
mistakes: I put all code in one 800-line script
I used absolute paths everywhere
I had no logging and spent hours adding print statements to find bugs
lessons: Use a src layout or app/ folder from the start
Configuration belongs in .env or YAML, not source code
Logging is not optional
optional_code_snippet_idea: recommended ML project folder structure with config.py using python-dotenv

title_seed: Understanding model drift and setting up automated retraining
experience_points: Concept drift is when the relationship between features and labels changes
Data drift is when input distribution shifts
KS-test and PSI detect drift in production data
Automated retraining triggered by drift reduced manual intervention
mistakes: I retrained on a schedule regardless of drift
I did not version the retraining data
I confused data drift with concept drift
lessons: Drift detection should trigger retraining, not a calendar schedule
Always version training datasets alongside model artifacts
Shadow deployment validates a retrained model before switching
optional_code_snippet_idea: KS-test drift detection script comparing production predictions to training distribution

title_seed: PostgreSQL for ML engineers - storing features, predictions, and logs
experience_points: A predictions table gave me the audit trail to debug model issues
Feature stores backed by PostgreSQL kept training and serving features consistent
SQLAlchemy ORM made switching between SQLite and Postgres seamless
Indexing on timestamp columns made queries 10x faster
mistakes: I stored JSON blobs in a text column instead of jsonb
I did not add timestamps to my tables
I ran raw string queries with user input
lessons: Use jsonb not text for JSON data in PostgreSQL
Always include created_at and updated_at columns
Parameterized queries are non-negotiable
optional_code_snippet_idea: SQLAlchemy model for a predictions table with jsonb features, predicted label, confidence, and timestamp

title_seed: How I used SHAP values to understand what my model was actually doing
experience_points: SHAP showed me that one feature was driving 80 percent of predictions and it was a leaked column
Waterfall plots made it easy to explain individual predictions to non-technical stakeholders
TreeExplainer is fast enough to run on every prediction in a batch job
Global SHAP summaries revealed the model relied heavily on a proxy variable
mistakes: I trusted feature importance from the model directly without cross-checking with SHAP
I ran KernelExplainer on a large dataset and it took 6 hours
I interpreted global SHAP values without looking at local explanations
lessons: Always verify feature importance with SHAP before deploying
Local explanations are as important as global ones for debugging
Explainability actively helps you find bugs, not just satisfy compliance
optional_code_snippet_idea: SHAP TreeExplainer with waterfall plot for a single prediction on a trained XGBoost model

title_seed: Building an end-to-end ML pipeline with Kubeflow
experience_points: Kubeflow Pipelines turns each ML step into a containerized component
The pipeline UI gives a visual DAG of every run with per-node logs
Caching pipeline steps saves hours when only the final training step changed
Parameterizing pipelines enables A/B experiments on hyperparameters at scale
mistakes: I underestimated how much Kubernetes knowledge Kubeflow requires
I did not version my pipeline components and a dependency update broke old runs
My first pipeline had no data validation step and bad data reached model training
lessons: Learn Kubernetes basics before touching Kubeflow
Add a data validation component as the very first pipeline step
Version component Docker images the same way you version model artifacts
optional_code_snippet_idea: Kubeflow pipeline component definition with input/output artifacts and containerized step

title_seed: How I handle imbalanced datasets in classification problems
experience_points: 99 percent accuracy on a fraud model meant nothing - it predicted everything as non-fraud
SMOTE improved recall from 12 percent to 61 percent
Class weights in sklearn are the quickest fix without data augmentation
Stratified k-fold ensures each fold has the same class ratio
mistakes: I applied SMOTE before splitting and leaked synthetic samples into the test set
I optimised for accuracy on an imbalanced dataset and the model was useless
I used random oversampling and the model just memorized duplicated samples
lessons: Always apply resampling inside the cross-validation loop, never before splitting
Pick your metric based on business cost not accuracy
Try class weights before SMOTE - it is simpler and often just as good
optional_code_snippet_idea: imbalanced-learn pipeline combining SMOTE with a classifier inside stratified cross-validation

title_seed: My experience with model versioning using DVC and Git
experience_points: DVC tracks large files like datasets and model artifacts outside of Git
dvc repro reruns only changed pipeline stages
Remote storage on S3 lets any teammate pull the exact dataset I used
Git tags on DVC pipeline runs gave a clean model performance history
mistakes: I committed a 500 MB model file to Git before knowing DVC existed
I forgot to push DVC artifacts and my teammate could not reproduce my results
I named DVC stages inconsistently and the pipeline DAG became unreadable
lessons: Set up DVC on day one before your dataset gets large
Always run dvc push after dvc repro
Treat DVC pipeline stages like functions - one clear input, one clear output
optional_code_snippet_idea: dvc.yaml pipeline with a train stage that takes processed data and outputs a model artifact

title_seed: How I optimised a slow ML inference API
experience_points: Loading the model on every request added 3 seconds of latency - loading at startup fixed it
Batching predictions reduced API calls by 90 percent
Model quantization with ONNX Runtime reduced memory by 60 percent with no accuracy loss
Async endpoints in FastAPI handled 10x more concurrent requests
mistakes: I loaded the model inside the prediction function - it reloaded on every request
I did not profile before optimising and worked on a step that contributed 2 percent of latency
I deployed a quantized model without running the full test suite
lessons: Always profile first - measure before you optimise
Load heavy artifacts at application startup, not at request time
Test quantized models with the same rigour as the original
optional_code_snippet_idea: FastAPI app with model loaded at startup using lifespan context and async prediction endpoint

title_seed: What I learned about hyperparameter tuning beyond grid search
experience_points: Bayesian optimisation finds better params in fewer trials than grid search
Optuna pruning stopped unpromising trials early and cut tuning time by 70 percent
Early stopping inside the objective function prevents wasting compute
Logging every trial to MLflow gave a full history to analyse which parameters mattered
mistakes: I ran a 500-combination grid search and the result was barely better than defaults
I tuned on the full training set without cross-validation
I treated tuning as a one-time step but optimal parameters shifted with new data
lessons: Start with random search before Bayesian optimisation
Always tune inside a cross-validation loop
Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis tells you which knobs actually matter
optional_code_snippet_idea: Optuna study with pruning callback, cross-validation objective, and MLflow integration

title_seed: How I built a real-time feature store for online ML predictions
experience_points: Pre-computing features in Redis reduced prediction latency from 800ms to 12ms
Keeping online and offline stores in sync was the biggest challenge
Point-in-time correct feature retrieval prevented future data leakage in training
Feature documentation saved the team from re-engineering the same features twice
mistakes: My first feature store had no TTL and memory ballooned to 40 GB overnight
I computed features differently in training and serving and got prediction skew
I had no monitoring on feature freshness and stale features caused a regression
lessons: Training and serving must use identical feature computation logic
Set TTLs on all feature keys and monitor cache hit rates
Feature stores are infrastructure - treat them like databases
optional_code_snippet_idea: Redis feature retrieval function with TTL management and fallback to batch-computed features

title_seed: What gradient boosting actually does and why it beats random forests on tabular data
experience_points: Gradient boosting builds trees sequentially each correcting errors of the previous
LightGBM is dramatically faster than XGBoost on large datasets
Early stopping prevents overfitting without manual epoch tuning
CatBoost handles categorical features natively without manual encoding
mistakes: I used too many trees without early stopping and overfit badly
I did not tune learning rate and tree depth together - they interact strongly
I assumed gradient boosting always beats simple models - on small data logistic regression won
lessons: Start with a low learning rate and more trees then use early stopping
Always have a simple baseline before gradient boosting
LightGBM leaf-wise growth can overfit on small datasets - use max_depth
optional_code_snippet_idea: LightGBM training with early stopping, cross-validation, and feature importance plot

title_seed: How I containerised a multi-model ML service with Docker Compose
experience_points: Docker Compose ran the API, model server, Redis, and Postgres locally with one command
Health checks prevented the API from starting before the model server was ready
Named volumes kept model artifacts persistent across container restarts
Environment variable substitution kept secrets out of the config file
mistakes: I hardcoded service hostnames as localhost - that breaks inside Docker networks
I did not set memory limits and the model container consumed all RAM
I used the default bridge network and services could not discover each other
lessons: Use service names as hostnames inside Docker Compose, not localhost
Always set resource limits on containers running large models
Use a named Docker network and define it explicitly in your compose file
optional_code_snippet_idea: docker-compose.yml with FastAPI, Redis, and model container with health checks

title_seed: My honest take on using Jupyter notebooks in production ML projects
experience_points: Notebooks are great for exploration but dangerous as production code
Hidden state from out-of-order cells caused bugs that took hours to trace
nbconvert converts notebooks to scripts for refactoring legacy code
Papermill enables parameterized notebook execution for scheduled reporting
mistakes: I shared a notebook as the deliverable and the next person could not reproduce it
I used global variables across cells and lost track of kernel state
I mixed exploration, cleaning, training, and evaluation in one 1500-line notebook
lessons: Use notebooks for exploration then refactor to modules before production
Restart and run all cells before sharing any notebook
One notebook should do one thing
optional_code_snippet_idea: Papermill notebook execution with injected parameters for automated reporting

title_seed: How I set up model A/B testing in a production ML system
experience_points: Shadow mode runs the new model in parallel without serving predictions to users
Traffic splitting at the API gateway is cleaner than inside the model service
Statistical significance testing determines when enough data has been collected
Logging model version with every prediction made rollback analysis straightforward
mistakes: I ran both models without consistent logging and could not attribute predictions
I ended the A/B test too early - the difference was noise not a real improvement
I did not define success metrics before the test started
lessons: Define success metrics and minimum detectable effect before starting
Log model version, prediction, confidence, and outcome for every request
Shadow deployment is safer than live traffic splitting for first comparisons
optional_code_snippet_idea: FastAPI middleware routing requests to model A or B by percentage with version logging

title_seed: What I learned about data quality and why it kills more ML projects than bad algorithms
experience_points: A silent schema change caused our model to predict noise for two weeks
Great Expectations gave automated data validation before every training job
Null value spikes were the most common root cause of sudden model degradation
Data profiling before training revealed distributions that made no business sense
mistakes: I assumed the data pipeline was correct and skipped validation
I checked data quality only once during EDA and never again in production
I trusted row counts as a quality signal - the rows were there but values were zeros
lessons: Add automated data validation to every pipeline stage
Row count checks are not enough - validate distributions, null rates, and value ranges
Treat data quality alerts with the same urgency as infrastructure alerts
optional_code_snippet_idea: Great Expectations checkpoint with null rate, value range, and column type expectations

title_seed: How I deployed a transformer model for NLP inference without a GPU
experience_points: ONNX export reduced the transformer model size by 40 percent
INT8 quantisation cut inference time from 1.2 seconds to 180ms on CPU
Hugging Face optimum made the export and quantisation pipeline straightforward
Caching tokenizer outputs for repeated inputs eliminated redundant computation
mistakes: I deployed the full PyTorch model on CPU with 4 second latency per request
I quantised without checking accuracy and it degraded on edge cases
I did not benchmark different batch sizes
lessons: Always benchmark CPU inference before deciding you need a GPU
Test quantised models thoroughly on your data distribution
ONNX Runtime speed improvement is real and consistent
optional_code_snippet_idea: Hugging Face model exported to ONNX with dynamic quantisation and ONNX Runtime inference

title_seed: How I used Airflow to orchestrate a daily ML training pipeline
experience_points: Airflow DAGs gave a visual history of every pipeline run with task-level logs
XComs passed model metrics between tasks so the deploy task could check quality first
Retries with exponential backoff handled intermittent upstream data delays
Sensors waited for upstream data before triggering training
mistakes: I put heavy computation in the DAG file and it caused the scheduler to lag
I used datetime.now() inside a task and got inconsistent behaviour during backfills
I did not set task timeouts and a hung download blocked the entire pipeline
lessons: Keep DAG files thin - delegate computation to operators or external scripts
Use execution_date not datetime.now() for time-dependent logic
Always set execution_timeout on every task
optional_code_snippet_idea: Airflow DAG with data sensor, training task, evaluation task, and conditional model promotion via XCom

title_seed: Lessons from building my first recommendation system
experience_points: Collaborative filtering works well with enough user interaction data
The cold start problem required a content-based fallback strategy
Implicit feedback needed different handling than explicit ratings
Offline NDCG metrics did not always correlate with online A/B test results
mistakes: I used RMSE to evaluate a recommendation model - wrong metric for ranking
I did not handle popularity bias and the system kept recommending the same top items
I trained on all historical data including old behaviour no longer relevant
lessons: Evaluate with ranking metrics like NDCG and MAP, not RMSE
Add diversity and novelty constraints to avoid filter bubbles
Recency matters - weight recent interactions more heavily
optional_code_snippet_idea: implicit library ALS model with confidence-weighted training on click data

title_seed: What I learned about logging and observability in ML systems
experience_points: Structured JSON logs made it possible to filter by model version in Elasticsearch
Logging prediction input hashes helped reproduce exact model behaviour
Correlation IDs tied a user request through API, feature store, model, and database
Log sampling at high traffic reduced storage cost without losing visibility
mistakes: I logged plain text strings and could not programmatically filter logs
I logged raw user input and created a privacy issue requiring an urgent hotfix
I had no log rotation and the disk filled up overnight taking the service down
lessons: Use structured logging from day one - JSON logs are queryable, plain strings are not
Never log raw user data or PII
Set up log rotation and retention policies before production
optional_code_snippet_idea: Python structured logging setup with correlation ID injection and JSON formatter

title_seed: How I used transfer learning to build a classifier with very little data
experience_points: Fine-tuning BERT on 500 labelled samples outperformed a custom model on 10000
Freezing early layers prevented catastrophic forgetting
Data augmentation via back-translation improved robustness
Learning rate warmup was essential to preserve pretrained weights
mistakes: I fine-tuned all layers with a high learning rate and the model forgot everything
I did not use a validation set and severely overfit the small dataset
I assumed a larger pretrained model always wins - a smaller one fine-tuned faster and scored higher
lessons: Use a very small learning rate when fine-tuning pretrained models
Freeze base layers first then gradually unfreeze
Small data problems are where transfer learning shines most
optional_code_snippet_idea: Hugging Face Trainer fine-tuning BERT classifier with frozen layers and learning rate warmup

title_seed: How Kubernetes changed the way I think about ML model deployment at scale
experience_points: Horizontal pod autoscaling handled traffic spikes without manual intervention
Rolling deployments let me update model versions with zero downtime
Resource requests and limits prevented one model from starving other services
Readiness probes ensured traffic only reached pods that had loaded the model
mistakes: I deployed without resource limits and one pod took down the entire node
I used Recreate instead of RollingUpdate and had 30 seconds of downtime
I did not configure liveness probes and a deadlocked pod kept receiving traffic
lessons: Always set CPU and memory requests and limits on model serving pods
Use readiness probes that actually check model health
Learn kubectl basics before deploying to production
optional_code_snippet_idea: Kubernetes Deployment manifest with readiness probe, resource limits, and rolling update strategy

title_seed: What I learned doing exploratory data analysis before every ML project
experience_points: EDA revealed that 30 percent of training data had an incorrectly computed label
Distribution plots caught a bimodal feature indicating two different customer segments
Correlation matrices pointed to multicollinear features before building a linear model
Time-based EDA showed seasonal patterns the model needed to account for
mistakes: I skipped EDA on a tight deadline and spent days debugging bad training data
I looked at averages only and missed outliers distorting the model
I did EDA once and never revisited it when new data arrived
lessons: Budget time for EDA on every project
Look at distributions not just means and medians
Re-run EDA when new data batches arrive
optional_code_snippet_idea: pandas-profiling report with automated distribution, correlation, and missing value analysis

title_seed: How I built a model training pipeline that runs on a schedule
experience_points: A weekly retraining cron job kept the model fresh without manual triggers
Locking the environment with a requirements hash prevented dependency drift
Slack notifications meant I never had to check training logs manually
Storing run metadata in a JSON file gave a lightweight audit trail
mistakes: My cron job ran as root and a bad script wiped a directory it should not have
I had no failure notification and the training job silently failed for two weeks
I ran training and serving on the same machine and training killed serving during peak traffic
lessons: Run scheduled jobs as a dedicated service account with minimal permissions
Always have failure alerting
Isolate training workloads from serving infrastructure completely
optional_code_snippet_idea: Python training script with file-based locking, Slack webhook notification, and metadata logging

title_seed: What I learned about model testing and why most ML projects skip it
experience_points: Unit testing feature engineering functions caught a bug that had been in production for months
Smoke tests on the model artifact checked output shapes before deployment
Behavioural tests checked that model output changed in the expected direction when input changed
Regression tests compared new model metrics against a baseline
mistakes: I deployed without automated tests and a silent type error caused wrong predictions
I only tested the happy path and missed edge cases like null feature values
I confused model evaluation with model testing - they are different and both necessary
lessons: Test feature engineering code the same way you test any software
Smoke tests on model outputs should be part of every CI pipeline
Behavioural tests are the most useful for catching subtle regressions
optional_code_snippet_idea: pytest suite with feature function unit tests, model output smoke test, and a behavioural test

title_seed: How I used Weights and Biases to manage large-scale ML experiments
experience_points: WandB sweeps ran 200 hyperparameter combinations overnight automatically
Artifact tracking linked every checkpoint to the exact dataset and config that produced it
Real-time loss curves let me kill bad runs early
Team dashboards gave everyone visibility into what had already been tried
mistakes: I logged too much data per step and slowed training by 30 percent
I did not name runs consistently and the dashboard became unreadable after 50 experiments
I forgot to set the project name and all runs went into a generic project
lessons: Log at epoch level by default - step-level logging is only worth it for debugging
Define a naming convention for runs before your experiment count reaches double digits
Use WandB artifacts for checkpoints so every model is traceable
optional_code_snippet_idea: WandB run init with config logging, per-epoch metric logging, and model artifact saving

title_seed: My experience building an ML model for time series forecasting
experience_points: Lag features and rolling statistics were more predictive than any complex architecture
Train/test split must respect temporal order - random splitting causes leakage
Walk-forward validation gave a realistic estimate of production performance
The model failed during a seasonal period it had never seen
mistakes: I used random cross-validation on time series and got 95 percent accuracy that fell to 60 in production
I did not check for autocorrelation in residuals
I trained on normalised data but forgot to inverse-transform predictions
lessons: Never use random splitting for time series - always split by time
Walk-forward validation is the standard for time series
Include at least two full seasonal cycles in your training data
optional_code_snippet_idea: walk-forward validation loop with lag feature generation and rolling mean features

title_seed: How I implemented a model confidence threshold to reduce bad predictions
experience_points: Low-confidence predictions were routed to a human review queue
Platt scaling made predicted probabilities reflect actual outcome frequencies
Monitoring low-confidence rates detected distribution shift before accuracy metrics did
Setting the threshold with a business cost function gave defensible justification
mistakes: I used raw softmax probabilities without calibration - they were systematically overconfident
I set the threshold based on intuition rather than optimising on a validation set
I had no fallback for low-confidence predictions
lessons: Always calibrate classification models before using probabilities as confidence scores
Optimise the confidence threshold against your specific business cost function
A well-designed abstention strategy is often more valuable than a slightly better model
optional_code_snippet_idea: Platt scaling with CalibratedClassifierCV and threshold selection using precision-recall curve

title_seed: What I learned about ML system design from reading production postmortems
experience_points: Most production ML failures are data failures not model failures
Tight coupling between the feature pipeline and model service caused cascading failures
Circuit breakers prevented a downstream data outage from taking down the API
Blameless postmortem culture led to fixing systemic problems instead of blaming individuals
mistakes: I designed a system with no fallback and a single point of failure took everything down
I never tested what happened when the model server was unavailable
I had no runbook and recovery during an incident took twice as long
lessons: Design for failure - assume every dependency will go down
Have a rule-based fallback for every ML-powered feature
Write runbooks before you need them, not during an incident
optional_code_snippet_idea: FastAPI endpoint with circuit breaker pattern and rule-based fallback when model service is down

title_seed: How I reduced ML model training time by 60 percent without changing the algorithm
experience_points: Moving data preprocessing out of the training loop and caching it cut 40 percent of time
Mixed precision training with float16 doubled GPU throughput with no accuracy drop
DataLoader workers eliminated CPU bottlenecks during data loading
Gradient accumulation gave a larger effective batch size without OOM errors
mistakes: I preprocessed data inside the forward pass and repeated the same computation every epoch
I used batch size 1 because I was afraid of memory issues - it was 10x slower
I enabled mixed precision without checking for NaN gradients and training diverged
lessons: Profile your training loop before optimising
Preprocess and cache data before training begins
Always monitor gradient norms when using mixed precision
optional_code_snippet_idea: PyTorch training loop with mixed precision, gradient accumulation, and multi-worker DataLoader

title_seed: What I learned about securing ML APIs and preventing model abuse
experience_points: Rate limiting prevented a scraping attack extracting training data through repeated calls
Input validation rejected adversarial inputs before reaching the model
Short-lived API key tokens reduced the blast radius of credential leaks
Logging inputs with a hash detected when someone was systematically probing the model
mistakes: I shipped a model API with no authentication and someone used it for their own product
I had no input size limits and a large request caused the prediction service to OOM
I logged raw prediction inputs containing personal data
lessons: Treat ML APIs as public-facing services with the same security standards
Validate and sanitise all inputs before they reach the model
Never log raw user inputs - hash or anonymise them first
optional_code_snippet_idea: FastAPI dependency for API key authentication with rate limiting and input size validation middleware

title_seed: How I built an automated model evaluation report that runs after every training job
experience_points: An automated HTML report after every training run replaced manual metric checking
The report included confusion matrix, classification report, and SHAP summary plots
Versioned report artifacts let me compare any two models side by side at any time
Metric threshold alerts flagged when a new model dropped below the baseline
mistakes: I generated reports manually and missed a regression for two weeks
My report was a raw CSV file that stakeholders could not read
I evaluated on the full test set without segmenting and missed poor subgroup performance
lessons: Automate your evaluation report - if it is not automated it will not be consistent
Segment evaluation by key subgroups not just overall metrics
Store evaluation reports as versioned artifacts alongside the model
optional_code_snippet_idea: Python script generating an HTML evaluation report with metrics, confusion matrix, and SHAP plots

title_seed: What choosing the wrong loss function cost me and how I learned to pick the right one
experience_points: MSE loss is sensitive to outliers - Huber loss made my regression model 20 percent more robust
Cross-entropy assumes mutually exclusive classes but I had multi-label data
Focal loss re-weighted hard examples and improved detection of rare events significantly
Custom loss functions in PyTorch are just differentiable Python functions
mistakes: I used MSE on a skewed distribution and the model ignored high-value minority cases
I used binary cross-entropy for a multi-class problem and debugged for days
I defined a custom loss that was not differentiable everywhere and training diverged
lessons: Match the loss function to your data distribution and business objective
Test custom loss functions with a numerical gradient check before full training
Switching loss functions often does more than tuning hyperparameters
optional_code_snippet_idea: PyTorch custom Huber loss and comparison with MSE on a regression task with outliers

title_seed: How I use pre-commit hooks and code quality tools to keep ML code clean
experience_points: Black formatting eliminated all style debates in code review
Ruff replaced flake8, isort, and pyupgrade with 10x faster execution
pre-commit hooks ran checks on every commit and kept bad code out of the repo
Type annotations caught a function signature mismatch during refactoring before CI
mistakes: I added pre-commit hooks to an existing project and had to fix 3000 style violations
I skipped type annotations and spent hours debugging a shape mismatch mypy would have caught
I configured Black and another formatter together and they kept reverting each other
lessons: Add formatting and linting tools at project start - retrofitting is painful
Use one formatter and stick to it
Type annotations in ML code are worth the effort
optional_code_snippet_idea: .pre-commit-config.yaml with Black, Ruff, and mypy hooks for a Python ML project
